name: Healthcare
description: Medical AI regulation covering clinical decision support, diagnostic AI, drug discovery, and patient data processing.

highRiskCategories.description: AI systems intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or which are themselves a product, covered by EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, including medical devices (MDR) and in vitro diagnostic medical devices (IVDR).

highRiskCategories.category: AI in education and vocational training for medical professionals
highRiskCategories.description: AI systems used to determine access to or influence outcomes in medical education and professional assessment.

sectorRequirements.requirement: AI systems qualifying as medical devices or accessories must obtain conformity assessment under the EU Medical Devices Regulation (MDR 2017/745) or equivalent national frameworks before market placement.
sectorRequirements.source: EU MDR Art. 52; EU AI Act Art. 6(1); FDA 21 CFR Part 820; MDA Act 2012 (Malaysia)

sectorRequirements.requirement: Clinical decision support AI must be validated against representative patient populations, with documented performance across demographic subgroups including age, sex, ethnicity, and comorbidity profiles.
sectorRequirements.source: FDA AI/ML Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) Framework; EU MDR Annex XIV; NICE Evidence Standards

sectorRequirements.requirement: Maintain human oversight for AI-assisted diagnostic and treatment recommendations, ensuring qualified healthcare professionals retain final decision authority over patient care.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 14; WHO Ethics & Governance of AI for Health; Malaysia MOH Clinical Practice Guidelines

sectorRequirements.requirement: Implement continuous post-market surveillance and real-world performance monitoring for deployed clinical AI systems, including adverse event reporting and model drift detection.
sectorRequirements.source: EU MDR Art. 83-86; FDA Post-Market Requirements; EU AI Act Art. 72

sectorRequirements.requirement: Ensure patient health data used for AI training and inference complies with healthcare data protection requirements, including consent management, anonymization standards, and cross-border transfer restrictions.
sectorRequirements.source: GDPR Art. 9 (health data); HIPAA (US); PDPA 2010 (Malaysia); EU AI Act Art. 10

sectorRequirements.requirement: Provide clinically meaningful explanations of AI diagnostic outputs that support, rather than replace, clinical reasoning, including confidence levels and key contributing factors.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 13; FDA Guidance on Clinical Decision Support; WHO AI Ethics Principles

sectorRequirements.requirement: Conduct health equity impact assessments before deploying AI systems that may affect access to or quality of healthcare for vulnerable populations.
sectorRequirements.source: UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation; OECD AI Principles; EU AI Act Recital 47; WHO Guidance

sectorRequirements.requirement: Document the predetermined change control plan for AI/ML-based medical devices, including the types of anticipated modifications and the methodology for implementing and validating updates.
sectorRequirements.source: FDA Predetermined Change Control Plan Guidance; EU MDR Art. 120; ISO 42001 Annex A

keyRisks.description: AI diagnostic tools trained predominantly on data from specific demographics may underperform for underrepresented patient populations, leading to misdiagnosis or delayed treatment.
Over-reliance on AI recommendations by healthcare professionals may erode clinical judgment skills and reduce critical evaluation of AI outputs.
AI systems processing large volumes of health data may enable re-identification of anonymized patient records through pattern inference.
Continuously learning medical AI systems may evolve beyond their original regulatory approval scope, creating compliance gaps.
Unclear allocation of liability between AI developers, healthcare institutions, and clinicians when AI-assisted decisions lead to adverse patient outcomes.
